name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
قُلْ إِنَّمَآ أَنَا۠ مُنذِرٌ ۖ وَمَا مِنْ إِلَـٰهٍ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ ٱلْوَٰحِدُ ٱلْقَهَّارُ (٦٥)
```

```english
Say, ‘I am just a warner, and there is no god except Allah, the One, the All-paramount, (65)
```

```arabic
رَبُّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْغَفَّـٰرُ (٦٦)
```

```english
the Lord of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them, the All-mighty, the All-forgiver.’ (66)
```

```arabic
قُلْ هُوَ نَبَؤٌا۟ عَظِيمٌ (٦٧)
```

```english
Say, ‘It is a great prophesy, (67)
```

```arabic
أَنتُمْ عَنْهُ مُعْرِضُونَ (٦٨)
```

```english
of which you are disregardful. (68)
```

```arabic
مَا كَانَ لِىَ مِنْ عِلْمٍۭ بِٱلْمَلَإِ ٱلْأَعْلَىٰٓ إِذْ يَخْتَصِمُونَ (٦٩)
```

```english
I have no knowledge of the Supernal Elite when they contend. (69)
```

```arabic
إِن يُوحَىٰٓ إِلَىَّ إِلَّآ أَنَّمَآ أَنَا۠ نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ (٧٠)
```

```english
All that is revealed to me is that I am just a manifest warner.’ (70)
```

```arabic
إِذْ قَالَ رَبُّكَ لِلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ إِنِّى خَـٰلِقٌۢ بَشَرًا مِّن طِينٍ (٧١)
```

```english
When your Lord said to the angels, ‘Indeed I am about to create a human being out of clay. (71)
```

```arabic
فَإِذَا سَوَّيْتُهُۥ وَنَفَخْتُ فِيهِ مِن رُّوحِى فَقَعُوا۟ لَهُۥ سَـٰجِدِينَ (٧٢)
```

```english
So when I have proportioned him and breathed into him of My spirit, then fall down in prostration before him.’ (72)
```

```arabic
فَسَجَدَ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ كُلُّهُمْ أَجْمَعُونَ (٧٣)
```

```english
Thereat the angels prostrated, all of them together, (73)
```

```arabic
إِلَّآ إِبْلِيسَ ٱسْتَكْبَرَ وَكَانَ مِنَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٧٤)
```

```english
but not Iblis; he acted arrogantly and he was one of the faithless. (74)
```

```arabic
قَالَ يَـٰٓإِبْلِيسُ مَا مَنَعَكَ أَن تَسْجُدَ لِمَا خَلَقْتُ بِيَدَىَّ ۖ أَسْتَكْبَرْتَ أَمْ كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلْعَالِينَ (٧٥)
```

```english
He said, ‘O Iblis! What keeps you from prostrating before that which I have created with My [own] two hands? Are you arrogant, or are you one of the exalted ones?’ (75)
```

```arabic
قَالَ أَنَا۠ خَيْرٌ مِّنْهُ ۖ خَلَقْتَنِى مِن نَّارٍ وَخَلَقْتَهُۥ مِن طِينٍ (٧٦)
```

```english
‘I am better than him,’ he said. ‘You created me from fire and You created him from clay.’ (76)
```

```arabic
قَالَ فَٱخْرُجْ مِنْهَا فَإِنَّكَ رَجِيمٌ (٧٧)
```

```english
He said, ‘Begone hence, for you are indeed an outcast, (77)
```

```arabic
وَإِنَّ عَلَيْكَ لَعْنَتِىٓ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلدِّينِ (٧٨)
```

```english
and indeed My curse will be on you till the Day of Retribution.’ (78)
```

```arabic
قَالَ رَبِّ فَأَنظِرْنِىٓ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ (٧٩)
```

```english
He said, ‘My Lord! Respite me till the day they will be resurrected.’ (79)
```

```arabic
قَالَ فَإِنَّكَ مِنَ ٱلْمُنظَرِينَ (٨٠)
```

```english
Said He, ‘You are indeed among the reprieved (80)
```

```arabic
إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْوَقْتِ ٱلْمَعْلُومِ (٨١)
```

```english
until the day of the known time.’ (81)
```

```arabic
قَالَ فَبِعِزَّتِكَ لَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ (٨٢)
```

```english
He said, ‘By Your might, I will surely pervert them, (82)
```

```arabic
إِلَّا عِبَادَكَ مِنْهُمُ ٱلْمُخْلَصِينَ (٨٣)
```

```english
except Your exclusive servants among them.’ (83)
```

```arabic
قَالَ فَٱلْحَقُّ وَٱلْحَقَّ أَقُولُ (٨٤)
```

```english
Said He, ‘The truth is that—and I speak the truth— (84)
```

```arabic
لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنكَ وَمِمَّن تَبِعَكَ مِنْهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ (٨٥)
```

```english
I will surely fill hell with you and all those who follow you.’ (85)
```

```arabic
قُلْ مَآ أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ وَمَآ أَنَا۠ مِنَ ٱلْمُتَكَلِّفِينَ (٨٦)
```

```english
Say, ‘I do not ask you any reward for it, and I am no impostor. (86)
```

```arabic
إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ لِّلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٨٧)
```

```english
It is just a reminder for all the nations, (87)
```

```arabic
وَلَتَعْلَمُنَّ نَبَأَهُۥ بَعْدَ حِينٍۭ (٨٨)
```

```english
and you will surely learn its tidings in due time.’ (88)
```

## (Commentary)

This is the final section of the Surah’s chapters, comprising the command to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to convey his warning and his call to monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts*. It establishes that turning away from the truth and following Satan leads man to the punishment of the Fire, which has been decreed against him and his followers. At this point, the Surah concludes.

His saying, the Exalted: **قُلْ إِنَّما أَنَا مُنْذِرٌ وَما مِنْ إِلهٍ إِلَّا اللهُ الْواحِدُ الْقَهَّارُ** (Say, ‘I am only a warner, and there is no god except Allah, the One, the All-paramount) until His saying **الْعَزِيزُ الْغَفَّارُ** (the All-mighty, the All-forgiver). In these two verses, the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is commanded to proclaim that he is a warner and that Allah, the Exalted, is One in His divinity. His saying, "I am only a warner" (إِنَّما أَنَا مُنْذِرٌ), serves to restrict his role to that of warning and to negate all other motives that people might associate with such a call, such as seeking wealth or status. This is further indicated at the end of the verses by his saying: **قُلْ ما أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ وَما أَنَا مِنَ الْمُتَكَلِّفِينَ** (Say, ‘I do not ask you any reward for it, nor am I one of those who fabricate’).

His saying: **وَما مِنْ إِلهٍ إِلَّا اللهُ** (and there is no god except Allah) until the end of the two verses is a proclamation of His monotheism (توحيده) through an argument derived from the attributes signified by His names.

His saying: **وَما مِنْ إِلهٍ إِلَّا اللهُ** is a negation of every deity (إله)—and the deity is the one truly worthy of worship (المعبود بالحق)—other than Him, the Exalted. As for the affirmation of His divinity, it is accepted as a given once the divinity of others is negated, for there is no dispute between Islam and polytheism (الشرك) regarding the fundamental existence of a Deity; rather, the dispute lies in whether the Deity—the one truly worthy of worship—is Allah, the Exalted, or someone else. Furthermore, the attributes mentioned in the two verses contain the affirmation of His divinity just as they serve as a proof for the negation of the divinity of any other than Him.

His saying: **الْواحِدُ الْقَهَّارُ** (the One, the All-paramount) indicates His uniqueness in His existence and His subjugation of all things. This is because He, the Exalted, is One (واحد); nothing resembles Him in His existence, and there is no limit to His perfection, which is identical to His Necessary Existence (وجوده الواجب). Thus, He is Self-Sufficient (الغني بذاته) in an absolute sense, while everything else is poor (فقير), needing Him from every aspect. Nothing possesses existence or the effects of existence except what He has bestowed and emanated. Therefore, He, the Glorified, is the Subduer (القاهر) of everything according to His will, and everything is obedient to Him in what He intends, humbled before Him in what He desires.

This essential humility (الخضوع الذاتي) is the reality of worship (العبادة). If it were permissible for anything in existence to be worshipped—by performing an act that represents servitude and humility—it would be the worship of Him, the Glorified. For everything assumed besides Him is subjugated and humbled before Him, possessing nothing for itself or for others, and having no independence in existence or the effects of existence. Thus, He, the Glorified, is the Deity truly worthy of worship, and none other.

His saying: **رَبُّ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَما بَيْنَهُمَا** (the Lord of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them) provides another proof for His uniqueness in divinity. This is because the system of governance (نظام التدبير) operating in the entire world is a single, connected system, neither partitioned nor fragmented. This is a sign of the unity of the Governor (المدبر). It has been mentioned repeatedly that creation (الخلق) and governance (التدبير) are inseparable.

Governance is creation from one perspective, just as creation is governance from another. The Creator who brought the heavens, the earth, and what is between them into existence is Allah, the Glorified—even according to the opponent. Therefore, He, the Exalted, is their Lord and their Governor. Consequently, He alone is the Deity who must be sought through worship, because worship is the representation of the worshipper's servitude and state of being owned (مملوكيته) in the face of the Mastership (مولوية) and Ownership (مالكية) of the Worshipped, and His disposal over the worshipper by bestowing blessings and averting calamities. Thus, He, the Glorified, is the Deity in the heavens, the earth, and what is between them; there is no god but He. Understand this well.

It is possible that His saying: **رَبُّ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَما بَيْنَهُمَا** serves as an explanation for His saying "the All-paramount" (الْقَهَّارُ) or "the One, the All-paramount" (الْواحِدُ الْقَهَّارُ).

His saying: **الْعَزِيزُ الْغَفَّارُ** (the All-mighty, the All-forgiver) provides yet another proof for His uniqueness in divinity. This is because He, the Exalted, is Mighty (عزيز); nothing can overcome Him by forcing Him into what He does not intend or preventing Him from what He intends. He is the Mighty in an absolute sense, and everything else is lowly (ذليل) before Him, devoutly obedient (قانت) to Him. Worship is the manifestation of lowliness, and it is only valid when directed toward Might (العزة), and there is no might for any other than Him except through Him.

Furthermore, the ultimate goal of worship—which is the representation of servitude—is seeking proximity to the Worshipped and removing the blemish of distance from the worshipping servant, which is the forgiveness of sin. Allah, the Glorified, is the sole possessor of the mercy whose treasures are never exhausted, and He is the one who admits His worshipping servants into the Abode of His Honor in the Hereafter. Thus, He is the All-Forgiver (الغفار) who must be worshipped out of hope for His forgiveness.

It is also possible that His saying: **الْعَزِيزُ الْغَفَّارُ** is an allusion to the reason for the call to monotheism or the necessity of faith in Him, understood from the context of His saying: **وَما مِنْ إِلهٍ إِلَّا اللهُ الْواحِدُ الْقَهَّارُ**. The meaning would be: I call you to His oneness, so believe in Him, for He is the Mighty who is untainted by any lowliness, the Forgiver of sins; and such must the Deity be.

His saying, the Exalted: **قُلْ هُوَ نَبَأٌ عَظِيمٌ أَنْتُمْ عَنْهُ مُعْرِضُونَ** (Say, ‘It is a great prophecy, from which you are turning away) The pronoun "It" (هو) refers to the discourse on divine unity mentioned in His saying: **وَما مِنْ إِلهٍ إِلَّا اللهُ** etc.

It has been said that the pronoun refers to the Quran; thus, it is the "great prophecy" from which they turned away. This is more consistent with the context of the preceding verses related to the matter of the Quran, and also more consistent with His subsequent saying: **ما كانَ لِي مِنْ عِلْمٍ بِالْمَلَإِ الْأَعْلى إِذْ يَخْتَصِمُونَ** (I had no knowledge of the Highest Assembly when they were contending), meaning: until the Quran informed me of it. It has also been said that it refers to the Day of Resurrection, but this is the most remote of the possibilities.

His saying, the Exalted: **ما كانَ لِي مِنْ عِلْمٍ بِالْمَلَإِ الْأَعْلى إِذْ يَخْتَصِمُونَ** (I had no knowledge of the Highest Assembly when they were contending). The **Highest Assembly** (الملأ الأعلى) refers to the company of angels. It seems the "contending" (اختصامهم) refers to what Allah alluded to in His saying: **إِذْ قالَ رَبُّكَ لِلْمَلائِكَةِ إِنِّي جاعِلٌ فِي الْأَرْضِ خَلِيفَةً** (When your Lord said to the angels, ‘Indeed I am going to set a viceroy on the earth’) until the end of the verses.

The meaning seems to be: I did not know of the contention of the Highest Assembly until Allah revealed it to me in His Book; for I am only a warner who follows the revelation (الوحي).

His saying, the Exalted: **إِنْ يُوحى إِلَيَّ إِلَّا أَنَّما أَنَا نَذِيرٌ مُبِينٌ** (Nothing is revealed to me except that I am a manifest warner). This is an emphasis of His saying: "I am only a warner" (إِنَّما أَنَا مُنْذِرٌ), and it serves as a justification for His saying: "I had no knowledge of the Highest Assembly." The meaning is: I did not know that because my knowledge does not come from myself; rather, it is through revelation, and nothing is revealed to me except what pertains to warning.

His saying, the Exalted: **إِذْ قالَ رَبُّكَ لِلْمَلائِكَةِ إِنِّي خالِقٌ بَشَراً مِنْ طِينٍ** (When your Lord said to the angels, ‘Indeed I am about to create a human out of clay’). What the context suggests is that this verse and those following it are not a continuation of the Prophet’s (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) speech: "I am only a warner," etc. The evidence for this is His saying: "your Lord" (رَبُّكَ). Thus, it is from the speech of Allah, the Exalted, referring to the time of the contention of the Highest Assembly. The adverbial phrase of time (الظرف) is connected to what His saying "when they were contending" (إِذْ يَخْتَصِمُونَ) is connected to, or it is connected to an omitted verb, the estimation being: "Mention when your Lord said to the angels," etc. For Allah’s saying to the angels: "Indeed I am going to set a viceroy on the earth" and His saying to them: "Indeed I am about to create a human out of clay" were concurrent, occurring at the same time.

Based on this, the meaning of His saying: "When your Lord said" (إِذْ قالَ رَبُّكَ), etc., resolves to something like our saying: Mention at that time when your Lord said such and such; for that was the time of their contention.

Some have considered His saying: "When your Lord said," etc., as an explanation for His saying: "when they were contending" (إِذْ يَخْتَصِمُونَ). They then took the "contending"—after interpreting it as an exchange of words—to encompass the entirety of Allah’s saying to the angels: "Indeed I am going to set a viceroy on the earth," their saying: "Will You set therein...?" etc., His speech to Adam and Adam’s speech to them, His saying to them: "Indeed I am about to create a human," the speech of Iblis, and Allah’s speech to him.

It was said that if "contending" is taken to mean a dispute, and the phrase "when they were contending" indicates that the dispute was among the angels themselves—not between them and Allah, the Glorified—then Allah’s informing them by His saying: "Indeed I am going to set a viceroy on the earth" and "Indeed I am about to create a human" was through the mediation of an angel. Likewise, His speech to Adam and Iblis. Thus, their saying to their Lord: "Will You set therein one who will cause corruption therein?" etc., would be their speech to the mediating angel, and the contention would occur among themselves.

However, as you know, none of what he mentioned can be inferred from the context of the verses.

His saying: **إِنِّي خالِقٌ بَشَراً مِنْ طِينٍ** (Indeed I am about to create a human out of clay). **Human** (البشر) refers to the person (الإنسان). Al-Raghib said: *al-bashar* is the outer skin, while *al-adamah* is the inner layer. This is what the general grammarians have said. He said: The human is referred to as *bashar* in consideration of the appearance of his skin without hair, unlike animals that have wool or fur. The word *bashar* is used for singular, plural, and dual; He, the Exalted, said: **أَنُؤْمِنُ لِبَشَرَيْنِ** (Shall we believe two humans?). In the Quran, every instance where the human is considered in terms of his physical body and outward appearance is specified with the word *bashar*. [End of quote].

The verse identifies the starting point of human creation as clay (الطين). In Surah al-Rum, it is dust (التراب); in Surah al-Hijr, it is a dry clay (صلصال) from a molded mud (حمإ مسنون); and in Surah al-Rahman, it is a dry clay like pottery (صلصال كالفخار). There is no contradiction, for these are different states of the original matter from which he was created, and in each instance, one of them is pointed out.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَإِذا سَوَّيْتُهُ وَنَفَخْتُ فِيهِ مِنْ رُوحِي فَقَعُوا لَهُ ساجِدِينَ** (So when I have proportioned him and breathed into him of My spirit, then fall down before him in prostration). **Proportioning** (تسوية) the human is the balancing of his limbs by arranging them one upon another and completing them into the form of a perfect human. **Breathing the spirit into him** (نفخ الروح فيه) is making him possess a living human soul (نفس). The attribution of the spirit to Him, the Exalted, is an attribution of honor (تشريفية). His saying: "then fall down" (فَقَعُوا) is a command derived from "falling," and it is contingent upon the proportioning and the breathing.

His saying, the Exalted: **فَسَجَدَ الْمَلائِكَةُ كُلُّهُمْ أَجْمَعُونَ** (So the angels prostrated, all of them together). This clearly indicates the prostration of the angels to him without exception.

His saying, the Exalted: **إِلَّا إِبْلِيسَ اسْتَكْبَرَ وَكانَ مِنَ الْكافِرِينَ** (except Iblis; he acted arrogantly and was one of the faithless). That is, Iblis acted arrogantly and did not prostrate to him, and he was before that among the disbelievers, as He, the Glorified, narrated from him in Surah al-Hijr: **لَمْ أَكُنْ لِأَسْجُدَ لِبَشَرٍ خَلَقْتَهُ مِنْ صَلْصالٍ مِنْ حَمَإٍ مَسْنُونٍ** (I am not one to prostrate to a human whom You have created out of a dry clay [drawn] from a molded mud) [al-Hijr: 33].

His saying, the Exalted: **قالَ يا إِبْلِيسُ ما مَنَعَكَ أَنْ تَسْجُدَ لِما خَلَقْتُ بِيَدَيَّ أَسْتَكْبَرْتَ أَمْ كُنْتَ مِنَ الْعالِينَ** (He said, ‘O Iblis! What kept you from prostrating before that which I have created with My [own] two hands? Were you arrogant, or were you [one] of the exalted?’). The attribution of his creation to the "hand" is for honor through special distinction, just as He said: "and breathed into him of My spirit." The use of the dual "two hands" (بِيَدَيَّ) is a metaphor for the complete attention given to his creation and fashioning, for a human only uses both hands for work that he deems important. Thus, His saying: "I created with My two hands" is like His saying: **مِمَّا عَمِلَتْ أَيْدِينا** (of what Our hands have wrought) [Ya-Sin: 71].

It has been said that "hand" means power (القدرة), and the dual is merely for emphasis, like His saying: **ثُمَّ ارْجِعِ الْبَصَرَ كَرَّتَيْنِ** (Then turn your vision again) [al-Mulk: 3], and a narration has been reported to this effect.

It has also been said that "two hands" refers to the blessings of this world and the Hereafter. It is also possible to interpret the "two hands" as the two origins of body and spirit, or form and meaning, or the two attributes of Majesty (الجلال) and Beauty (الجمال); however, these are meanings for which there is no evidence from the literal wording.

His saying: **أَسْتَكْبَرْتَ أَمْ كُنْتَ مِنَ الْعالِينَ** is a reproachful interrogation. That is: Was your failure to prostrate because you acted arrogantly [now], or were you among those who are exalted (العالين)—meaning those whose rank is too high to be commanded to prostrate? For this reason, some have inferred from the verse that the "exalted" are a group of His creation who are so absorbed in their orientation toward their Lord that they are unaware of anything other than Him, the Exalted.

It has been said that "exaltation" (العلو) means arrogance, as in His saying: **وَإِنَّ فِرْعَوْنَ لَعالٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ** (and indeed Pharaoh was a tyrant in the land) [Yunus: 83]. The meaning would then be: Did you act arrogantly when you were commanded to prostrate, or were you previously among the arrogant?

This is refuted by the fact that it does not suit the requirements of the context; for the context requires the objective of ascertaining the root of his arrogance, not determining whether his arrogance was old or new.

It has also been said that the "exalted" refers to the angels of heaven, for those commanded to prostrate were the angels of the earth. This is refuted by the generality found in the verse.

His saying, the Exalted: **قالَ أَنَا خَيْرٌ مِنْهُ خَلَقْتَنِي مِنْ نارٍ وَخَلَقْتَهُ مِنْ طِينٍ** (He said, ‘I am better than him: You created me from fire and You created him from clay’). This is a justification for his failure to prostrate based on his claim of the nobility of his essence—that because his creation was from fire, he is better than Adam, who was created from clay. In this is an allusion that the divine command is only to be obeyed if it is "right" (حق) and not for its own sake, and that His command to prostrate to him was not right. This resolves to a denial of the absolute Sovereignty and Wisdom of Him, the Exalted, which is the root from which every sin originates. For sin only occurs by exiting the rule of His servitude and ownership, and by turning away from the fact that leaving the sin is better than committing it.

His saying, the Exalted: **قالَ فَاخْرُجْ مِنْها فَإِنَّكَ رَجِيمٌ وَإِنَّ عَلَيْكَ لَعْنَتِي إِلى يَوْمِ الدِّينِ** (He said, ‘Then get out of it, for you are indeed an outcast, and My curse shall lie on you until the Day of Retribution’). **Outcast** (الرجم) means being driven away, and the **Day of Retribution** (يوم الدين) is the Day of Recompense.

Regarding His saying: **وَإِنَّ عَلَيْكَ لَعْنَتِي** (and My curse shall lie on you), while in Surah al-Hijr it is: **وَإِنَّ عَلَيْكَ اللَّعْنَةَ** (and the curse shall lie on you) [verse 35]. It has been said regarding this: If the definite article (اللام) is for specific reference (للعهد), then there is no difference between the two expressions. If it is for the genus (للجنس), it is likewise the same, because the cursing of others—from the angels and humans—upon him is only a true expulsion and distancing from mercy if it is by the command of Allah and His distancing him from His mercy.

His saying, the Exalted: **قالَ رَبِّ فَأَنْظِرْنِي إِلى يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ** (He said, ‘My Lord! Respite me until the day they are resurrected’) until His saying **إِلى يَوْمِ الْوَقْتِ الْمَعْلُومِ** (until the day of the known time). The apparent change in the limit (الغاية) between the request and the answer—where he said: "until the day they are resurrected" and was answered with: "until the day of the known time"—indicates that what was granted is different from what he requested. It is inevitably the last day in which people disobey their Lord, which is before the Day of Resurrection. It appears that "Day" (اليوم) refers to the time-period (الظرف), so its addition to "Time" (الوقت) serves as emphasis.

His saying, the Exalted: **قالَ فَبِعِزَّتِكَ لَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ إِلَّا عِبادَكَ مِنْهُمُ الْمُخْلَصِينَ** (He said, ‘By Your might, I will surely pervert them all, except Your devoted servants among them’). The particle *ba* in **فَبِعِزَّتِكَ** is for an oath; he swore by His might that he would surely pervert them all, and he excepted the devoted ones (المخلصين)—those whom Allah has purified for Himself, such that neither Iblis nor anyone else has any share in them.

His saying, the Exalted: **قالَ فَالْحَقُّ وَالْحَقَّ أَقُولُ لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنْكَ وَمِمَّنْ تَبِعَكَ مِنْهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ** (He said, ‘This is the truth, and the truth I speak: I will surely fill hell with you and with those of them who follow you, all together’). This is the response of Him, the Exalted, to Iblis, and it contains the decree (القضاء) of the Fire against him and those who follow him.

His saying: **فَالْحَقُ** is a subject (مبتدأ) whose predicate (خبر) is omitted, or a predicate whose subject is omitted. The particle *fa* is for the ordering of what follows upon what preceded. "The Truth" (الحق) refers to that which is opposite to falsehood, as supported by the repetition of "the Truth" with the definite article, which certainly refers to the opposite of falsehood. The estimation is: "The Truth is what I swear by: I will surely fill hell..." or "My word is the Truth: I will surely fill..."

His saying: **وَالْحَقَّ أَقُولُ** (and the truth I speak) is a parenthetical sentence indicating the inevitability of the decree. It refutes what was alluded to by Iblis’s saying: "I am better than him," etc., regarding Allah’s command to prostrate being other than "right." The advancement of "the Truth" in "and the Truth I speak" and its being defined with the article is to signify restriction (الحصر).

His saying: **لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنْكَ وَمِمَّنْ تَبِعَكَ مِنْهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ** is the text of the decree that He passed. It seems that "from you" (مِنْكَ) refers to the genus of devils (الشياطين) so as to include Iblis, his progeny, and his tribe. His saying: "and with those of them who follow you" refers to the humans from the progeny of Adam.

We have provided extensive discussion on similar verses in Surah al-Hijr and on the story in Surahs al-Baqarah, al-A'raf, and al-Isra; so you should refer to them.

His saying, the Exalted: **قُل| ما أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ وَما أَنَا مِنَ الْمُتَكَلِّفِينَ** (Say, ‘I do not ask you any reward for it, nor am I one of those who fabricate’). This is a return to what was mentioned at the beginning of the Surah and throughout its verses: that the Quran is a reminder and that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is nothing but a warner. It is a refutation of what they cast at him with their saying: "Go on and stand by your gods! This is indeed something intended."

His saying: **ما أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ** means any worldly reward of wealth or status. His saying: **وَما أَنَا مِنَ الْمُتَكَلِّفِينَ** means I am not among the people of affectation (التكلف)—which is pretension and adorning oneself with what one does not possess.

His saying, the Exalted: **إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ لِلْعالَمِينَ** (It is just a reminder for all the nations). That is, the Quran is a general reminder for all the worlds (العالمين)—for the groups of people, the various nations, tribes, and others. It is not exclusive to one people over another such that wealth should be taken for its recitation or a reward for its teaching; rather, it is for everyone.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَتَعْلَمُنَّ نَبَأَهُ بَعْدَ حِينٍ** (And you will surely come to know its tidings after a while). That is, you will surely know what the Quran has informed of—the promise, the threat, its triumph over other religions, and so on—after a while (بَعْدَ حِينٍ), meaning after the passage of time.

It has been said that "after a while" refers to the Day of Resurrection; it has been said it refers to the day of death; and it has been said it refers to the Day of Badr. It is not far-fetched to say that its "tidings" (نبأه) are diverse and not restricted to any one of these days such that it alone would be intended; rather, it refers to the absolute sense, for each type of its tidings has its own time.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, with its chain of narration from Isma'il al-Ju'fi from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him): In a hadith mentioning the Ascension (المعراج), from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): **قال تعالى : يا محمد. قلت : لبيك يا رب. قال : فيما اختصم الملأ الأعلى؟ قال : قلت : سبحانك لا علم لي إلا ما علمتني. قال : فوضع يده أي يد القدرة بين ثديي ـ فوجدت بردها بين كتفي ـ قال : فلم يسألني عما مضى ولا عما بقي إلا علمته. فقال : يا محمد فيم اختصم الملأ الأعلى؟ قال : قلت : في الكفارات والدرجات والحسنات** — "Allah, the Exalted, said: 'O Muhammad.' I said: 'At Your service, O Lord.' He said: 'Regarding what did the Highest Assembly contend?' I said: 'Glory be to You! I have no knowledge except what You have taught me.' He said: 'Then He placed His hand'—meaning the hand of power—'between my breasts, and I felt its coolness between my shoulders.' He said: 'Then He did not ask me about what has passed nor what remains except that I knew it.' He said: 'O Muhammad, regarding what did the Highest Assembly contend?' I said: 'Regarding the expiations (الكفارات), the degrees (الدرجات), and the good deeds (الحسنات).'"

In *al-Majma'*, Ibn Abbas narrated from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) who said: **قال لي ربي : أتدري فيم يختصم الملأ الأعلى؟ فقلت : لا. قال : اختصموا في الكفارات والدرجات ـ فأما الكفارات فإسباغ الوضوء في السبرات ـ ونقل الأقدام إلى الجماعات ـ وانتظار الصلاة بعد الصلاة ، وأما الدرجات فإفشاء السلام وإطعام الطعام ـ والصلاة بالليل والناس نيام** — "My Lord said to me: 'Do you know regarding what the Highest Assembly contends?' I said: 'No.' He said: 'They contended regarding the expiations and the degrees. As for the expiations, they are: performing ablution thoroughly in the cold, walking to the congregational prayers, and waiting for one prayer after another. As for the degrees, they are: spreading peace (السلام), feeding the hungry, and praying at night while people are asleep.'"

I say: It was also narrated in *al-Khisal* from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), where he made what he interpreted as expiations an interpretation for degrees and vice versa. It was also narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur*—the hadith of *al-Majma'*—through many chains from several companions from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) with variations in the narrations.

In any case, the context of the verse refuses to align with the content of these narrations, and there is no evidence indicating that the narrations are in the position of interpreting the verse. Perhaps the contention mentioned in them is other than the contention mentioned in the verse.

In *Nahj al-Balagha*: **الحمد لله الذي لبس العز والكبرياء ـ واختارهما لنفسه دون خلقه ، وجعلهما حمى وحرما على غيره ، واصطفاهما لجلاله ، وجعل اللعنة على من نازعه فيهما من عباده ، ثم اختبر بذلك ملائكته المقربين ـ ليميز المتواضعين منهم من المستكبرين ـ فقال سبحانه ـ وهو العالم بمضمرات القلوب ومحجوبات الغيوب : (إِنِّي خالِقٌ بَشَراً مِنْ طِينٍ ـ فَإِذا سَوَّيْتُهُ وَنَفَخْتُ فِيهِ مِنْ رُوحِي فَقَعُوا لَهُ ساجِدِينَ ـ فَسَجَدَ الْمَلائِكَةُ كُلُّهُمْ أَجْمَعُونَ إِلَّا إِبْلِيسَ) ـ اعترضته الحمية فافتخر على آدم بخلقه ـ وتعصب عليه بأصله ـ. فعدو الله إمام المتعصبين وسلف المستكبرين ـ الذي وضع أساس العصبية ، ونازع الله رداء الجبرية ، وأدرع لباس التعزز ، وخلع قناع التذلل ـ ألا ترون كيف صغره الله بتكبره ، ووضعه بترفعه ـ فجعله في الدنيا مدحورا ، وأعد له في الآخرة سعيرا** — "Praise be to Allah who clothed Himself in Might and Grandeur, and chose them for Himself to the exclusion of His creation. He made them a sanctuary and a forbidden zone for others, and selected them for His Majesty. He placed a curse upon whoever among His servants disputes with Him regarding them. Then He tested His near angels therewith, to distinguish the humble among them from the arrogant. So He, the Glorified—and He is the Knower of the secrets of hearts and the hidden things of the Unseen—said: 'Indeed I am about to create a human out of clay. So when I have proportioned him and breathed into him of My spirit, then fall down before him in prostration. So the angels prostrated, all of them together, except Iblis.' Zealotry (الحمية) overcame him, so he boasted over Adam because of his creation and was prejudiced against him because of his origin. Thus, the enemy of Allah is the leader of the prejudiced and the predecessor of the arrogant, who laid the foundation of zealotry, disputed with Allah for the robe of Omnipotence, donned the garment of self-exaltation, and cast off the mask of humility. Do you not see how Allah belittled him for his pride and lowered him for his self-elevation? So He made him an outcast in this world and prepared for him a blazing fire in the Hereafter." [From a sermon].

In *al-Uyun*, with its chain to Muhammad ibn Ubaydah, who said: **سألت الرضا عليه‌السلام عن قول الله تعالى لإبليس : «ما مَنَعَكَ أَنْ تَسْجُدَ لِما خَلَقْتُ بِيَدَيَ» قال : يعني بقدرتي وقوتي** — "I asked Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah, the Exalted, to Iblis: 'What kept you from prostrating before that which I have created with My [own] two hands?' He said: 'It means by My power and My strength.'"

I say: A similar narration was reported in *al-Tawhid*, with its chain from Muhammad ibn Muslim from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him).

Regarding the story, there are other narrations that we have cited in their respective places in Surahs al-Baqarah, al-A'raf, al-Hijr, and al-Isra; so refer to them.

From *Jawami' al-Jami'*, from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): **للمتكلف ثلاث علامات : ينازع من فوقه ، ويتعاطى ما لا ينال ، ويقول ما لا يعلم** — "For the fabricator (المتكلف) there are three signs: he disputes with those above him, he reaches for what cannot be attained, and he says what he does not know."

I say: A similar narration was reported in *al-Khisal* from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) from Luqman in his testament to his son. It was also narrated through Sunni channels, and in some narrations: "he contends with those above him" (ينازل من فوقه).
